48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179

181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.

44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

8	/-!
9	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
10	
11	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
12	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
13	base change on the target.
14	
15	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
16	```
17	  X' --g'--> X
18	  |f'        |f
19	  v          v
20	  S' --g---> S
21	```
22	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
23	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
24	
25	The three main declarations are:
26	
27	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
28	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
29	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
31	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
32	  associativity).
33	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
34	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
35	
36	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
37	
38	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
39	Tag 02KH.
40	-/
41	
42	universe u
43	
44	open CategoryTheory Limits
45	
46	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
47	
48	open Scheme.Modules
49	
50	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
51	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
52	
53	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
54	
55	Given a commutative square
56	```
57	  X' --g'--> X
58	  |f'        |f
59	  v          v
60	  S' --g---> S
61	```
62	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
63	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
64	
65	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
66	```
67	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
68	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
69	```
70	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
71	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
72	commutativity of the square.
73	
74	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
75	base-change diagram. -/
76	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
77	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
78	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
79	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
80	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
81	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
82	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
83	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)
84	
85	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — locality of isomorphisms for `Scheme.Modules`
86	
87	Mathlib provides the per-open criterion `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`
88	(`IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`) and the stalkwise criterion
89	`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` for `TopCat.Sheaf`-valued morphisms,
90	but it does not package the stalk-local criterion at the level of `Scheme.Modules`
91	morphisms. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they are the locality tools needed for
92	the affine reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (where one checks the
93	base-change map after restricting to / taking stalks over affine opens). -/

97	abelian presheaves is a stalkwise isomorphism. Project-local: Mathlib only ships the
98	per-open criterion and the `TopCat.Sheaf`-level stalk criterion separately. -/

102	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by

104	  · intro h x
105	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
106	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
107	  · intro h
108	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
109	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
110	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
111	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
112	    haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := h
113	    haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
114	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
115	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
116	    exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
117	
118	/-- **Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** If `B` is a basis of
119	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
120	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
121	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:
122	Mathlib provides the stalkwise pieces (`germ_exist_of_isBasis`,
123	`stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis`) but not the packaged criterion at the
124	`Scheme.Modules` level. -/
125	theorem Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
126	    {ι : Type*} {B : ι → X.Opens} (hB : TopologicalSpace.Opens.IsBasis (Set.range B))
127	    (φ : M ⟶ N) (h : ∀ i, IsIso (φ.app (B i))) : IsIso φ := by
128	  -- Reduce to a stalkwise isomorphism of the underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism `α`.
129	  rw [Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map]
130	  intro x
131	  -- `α.app (op (B i))` is definitionally `φ.app (B i)`, hence an isomorphism on each basic open.
132	  have happ : ∀ U ∈ Set.range B,
133	      IsIso (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U)) := by
134	    rintro U ⟨i, rfl⟩; exact h i
135	  rw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
136	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
137	  · -- Injectivity of the stalk map from injectivity on a basis.
138	    refine TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis hB ?_ x
139	    intro U hU
140	    haveI := happ U hU
141	    exact (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
142	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).injective
143	  · -- Surjectivity: a germ at `x` comes from a section over a basic open, where `α` is onto.

146	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist_of_isBasis hB N.presheaf x t

149	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).surjective s

152	    rw [hs']
153	    rfl

155	/-- **Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism
156	`φ : M ⟶ N` of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism iff it restricts to an isomorphism on
157	the sections over every affine open of `X`. This is the precise reduction used in the

159	and over an affine open the base-change map becomes the pure ring-theoretic

161	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
162	    (φ : M ⟶ N) : IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U : X.affineOpens, IsIso (φ.app U) := by
163	  refine ⟨fun _ U => inferInstance, fun h => ?_⟩
164	  exact Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis
165	    (B := (Subtype.val : X.affineOpens → X.Opens))
166	    (by simpa [Subtype.range_val] using X.isBasis_affineOpens) φ h

367	      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.map
368	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom] :
369	    IsIso (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app N.fromTildeΓ (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)) := by
370	  -- The underlying function of `Hom.app` agrees (by `rfl`) with that of the `modulesSpecToSheaf`
371	  -- image, where the tilde–Γ triangle identity `toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app` applies.
372	  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
373	  have hfun : ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom
374	        (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app N.fromTildeΓ (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))) =
375	      ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom
376	        ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map N.fromTildeΓ).1.app (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)))) :=
377	    rfl
378	  rw [hfun]
379	  -- Abbreviations: `j` is the tilde localization map, `ρ` the structure-sheaf restriction, both
380	  -- localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)` at `powers a`; `L` is the section map of the counit.
381	  set j := (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤))
382	    (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)).hom with hj
383	  set ρ := ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.map
384	    (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom with hρ
385	  haveI hρinst : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers a) ρ := by rw [hρ]; infer_instance

388	    (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))).hom with hL
389	  -- The triangle identity gives `L ∘ₗ j = ρ`.
390	  have htri : L ∘ₗ j = ρ := by
391	    have := Scheme.Modules.toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app N (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)
392	    apply_fun ModuleCat.Hom.hom at this
393	    simpa [hL, hj, hρ, ModuleCat.hom_comp] using this
394	  -- `L` equals the canonical iso between the two localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)`, hence bijective.
395	  set ej := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) j with hej
396	  set eρ := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) ρ with heρ

399	    ext x

404	    IsLocalizedModule.ext (Submonoid.powers a) j (IsLocalizedModule.map_units ρ)

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
96	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
97	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
98	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
99	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
100	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
101	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
102	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
103	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
104	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
105	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
106	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{lemma}\leanok
110	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
111	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
113	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
114	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
115	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
116	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
117	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
118	  isomorphism.
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
123	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
124	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
125	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
126	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
127	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
128	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
129	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
130	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
131	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
132	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
133	\end{proof}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
137	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
139	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
140	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
142	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
147	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
148	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
149	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
150	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
151	  affine opens.
152	\end{proof}
153	
154	\subsection{The affine computation}
155	
156	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
157	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
158	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
159	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
160	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
161	
162	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
163	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
164	their proof sketches.
165	
166	\begin{lemma}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
170	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
172	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
173	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
174	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
175	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
176	  \[
177	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
178	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
179	  \]
180	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
181	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
182	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{proof}
186	
187	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
188	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
189	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
190	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
191	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
192	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
193	\end{proof}
194	
195	\begin{lemma}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
199	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
201	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
202	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
203	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
204	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
205	  \[
206	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
207	    \;\cong\;
208	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
209	  \]
210	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
211	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
212	  of \(N\).
213	\end{lemma}
214	
215	\begin{proof}
216	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
217	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
218	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
219	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
220	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
221	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
222	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
223	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
224	  equation of
225	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
226	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
227	\end{proof}
228	
229	\begin{lemma}
230	
231	\leanok
232	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
233	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
235	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
236	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
237	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
238	  \[
239	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
240	    \;\cong\;
241	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
242	  \]
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
247	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
248	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
249	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
250	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
251	\end{proof}

253	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
254	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
255	on their proof sketches.

257	\begin{lemma}

261	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}

373	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
374	    \;\cong\;
375	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
376	  \]
377	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
378	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
379	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
380	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
381	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
382	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
383	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
384	
385	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
386	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
387	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
388	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
389	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
390	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
391	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
396	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars}
397	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
398	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
399	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
400	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
401	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
402	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
403	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
404	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
405	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
406	
407	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
408	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
409	  \[
410	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
411	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
412	  \]
413	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
414	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
415	
416	  \medskip
417	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
418	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
419	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
420	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
421	
422	  \medskip
423	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
424	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
425	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
426	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
427	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
428	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
429	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
430	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
431	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
432	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
433	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
434	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
435	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
436	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
437	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
438	  an isomorphism.
439	
440	  \medskip
441	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
442	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
443	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
444	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
445	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
446	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
447	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
448	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
449	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
450	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
451	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
452	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
453	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
454	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
455	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
456	
457	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
458	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
459	  isomorphism
460	  \[
461	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
462	      \;\cong\;
463	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
464	  \]
465	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
466	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
467	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
468	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
469	  the \(\top\)-level one of
470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
471	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
472	  \(\top\).
473	
474	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
475	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
476	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
477	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
478	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
479	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
480	  \[
481	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
482	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
483	    \;=\;
484	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
485	  \]
486	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
487	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
488	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
489	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
490	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
491	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).
492	
493	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
494	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) is canonically the
495	  \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\)
496	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse
497	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
498	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
499	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
500	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
501	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
502	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
503	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
504	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
505	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
506	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
508	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
509	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
510	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
511	  global ring map.
512	
513	  \medskip
514	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
515	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
516	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
517	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
518	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
519	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
520	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
521	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
522	  needed for the affine lane.
523	\end{proof}
524	
525	\begin{remark}
526	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
527	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
528	  \begin{enumerate}
529	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
530	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
531	      \(R\)-module of sections of
532	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
533	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
534	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
535	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
536	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
537	      closed under isomorphism; hence
538	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
539	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
540	      needed for the affine lane.
541	  \end{enumerate}
542	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
543	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
544	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
545	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
546	\end{remark}
